articles.title: Salesloft AI Features: Rhythm and Signal-to-Action AI
articles.h1: Salesloft AI Features: Rhythm and Signal-to-Action AI
articles.metaDescription: A buyer's guide to Salesloft AI features: how Conductor AI, Rhythm, signals, and Plays turn buyer behavior into ranked, explained next-best actions for sellers.
articles.ogTitle: Salesloft AI Features: Rhythm and Conductor AI Explained
articles.ogDescription: How Salesloft's Conductor AI and Rhythm prioritize seller actions from buyer signals — and what to check before you trust it to act.
articles.heroLede: Salesloft's AI centers on Conductor AI, a prioritization engine that turns buyer signals into ranked, explained next-best actions inside the Rhythm workflow. Here's what it does, where it acts, and what to verify before you trust it.
articles.bodyHtml:
What Salesloft's AI actually is

Salesloft (now operating as Clari + Salesloft after a merger that closed in December 2025) builds sales-engagement software for outbound and follow-up. Its AI story has one center of gravity: Conductor AI, a patent-pending prioritization engine, and Rhythm, the seller workflow that engine powers.

In plain terms, Conductor AI reads buyer behavior and translates it into a ranked list of next-best actions for each individual seller. Rhythm is where those actions show up — a prioritized task queue that tells a rep what to do next and in what order. The promise is less guesswork about who to call and what to send, and more "here is the highest-impact thing on your plate right now."

How Conductor AI prioritizes and learns

Salesloft says Conductor AI weighs three dimensions when it ranks actions: data (seller activity, buyer engagement, and opportunity details such as deal size and stage), time (urgency and impact), and patterns (learning from outcomes to adjust how it prioritizes going forward). It re-prioritizes the Rhythm workflow in real time as new signals arrive, and continuously learns from which actions tend to drive successful outcomes.

It can also draft work, not just rank it. Conductor AI can automatically generate individualized follow-up emails inside Cadences based on a buyer's activity, interests, role, and company — drafts the seller can then edit before sending.

Signals and Plays: where the actions come from

The fuel for all of this is signals — external events sent to Salesloft from integrated applications. Signals appear in the Rhythm Task Feed and the Salesloft Live Feed. When a signal matches the criteria of a registered Play, Salesloft generates a task and places it in the seller's queue. Salesloft's signal-based selling ingests buyer signals from ecosystem partners including G2, Highspot, Seismic, UserGems, LeanData, and Vidyard, and can also ingest custom signals via API.

One detail matters for trust: each prioritized action in Rhythm comes with an explanation of why it's recommended, and the triggering signal is shown to the seller as the task's justification. That transparency is the difference between a queue you follow blindly and one you can reason about.

Reading Conductor AI through a bounded-autonomy lens

At campaignautomation.ai, we evaluate AI features by a simple test we call Trigger, Action, Impact: what event set the AI off, what did it do, and what changed as a result — each step visible and reversible. Salesloft's design maps onto this unusually cleanly. The trigger is a signal matched against a Play. The action is a task placed in Rhythm or a draft email written into a Cadence. The impact is whatever the seller does next.

The thing worth noticing — and what we'd call a sign of bounded autonomy — is where the AI stops. Conductor AI ranks and drafts; the seller still decides and sends. The displayed signal and the "why" behind each task give you a built-in audit trail. When you evaluate this against your own guardrails, the questions to ask are practical: which Plays are registered, what signals can create tasks, and who reviews auto-drafted emails before they leave. The engine learns from outcomes, so the Plays and criteria you set become the boundary that shapes what it surfaces over time.

Last reviewed June 2026. Salesloft updates its AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on the vendor's own site.

What's current, and what's changing

For buyers, the naming is stable where it counts: Conductor AI and Rhythm are the active, current brand names with no rename. The 2025–2026 changes are corporate and conversational rather than a teardown of the prioritization engine. Salesloft merged with Clari (closed December 2025); the combined entity operates as "Clari + Salesloft" and has not yet announced a final company name.

On the conversational side, in March 2026 Clari + Salesloft announced it will gradually sunset the acquired Drift conversational marketing product and partner exclusively with 1mind to provide AI "Superhuman" digital teammates. These agents engage buyers across channels — websites, product, deal rooms, and video conferences — qualify leads, and generate structured signals that flow into Salesloft Cadences and revenue forecasting. The takeaway: if conversational engagement is part of your evaluation, Drift is on the way out and 1mind is the replacement, while Conductor AI and Rhythm carry on as the core engagement intelligence.

Start here

Before you let Conductor AI rank your reps' day, know your guardrails

A prioritization engine is only as safe as the Plays and signals you let drive it. Our free Readiness Score shows you where you can hand work to AI and where a human still needs to hold the pen — 4 minutes, no login.

Get your free Readiness Score →

  • AI sales automation — how signal-to-action workflows fit into a broader revenue motion.
  • What is bounded autonomy — the framework we used to evaluate where Conductor AI should and shouldn't act.

articles.title: Mailchimp AI Features: A Guide to Intuit Assist for Marketers
articles.h1: Mailchimp AI features: a guide to Intuit Assist for marketers
articles.metaDescription: A buyer's guide to Mailchimp AI features: what Intuit Assist, Analytics AI, predictive analytics, and send-time optimization do, and how to evaluate them.
articles.ogTitle: Mailchimp AI Features: A Guide to Intuit Assist
articles.ogDescription: What Mailchimp's Intuit Assist, Analytics AI, and predictive features do for marketers — and how to evaluate them with a bounded-autonomy lens.
articles.heroLede: Mailchimp now packages most of its AI under the Intuit Assist brand, joined by predictive analytics and a new conversational analytics agent. Here's what each piece does — and the questions to ask before you let any of it act on your account.
articles.bodyHtml:
What Mailchimp's AI is today

Mailchimp's AI is delivered chiefly under the Intuit Assist for Mailchimp brand — a generative AI marketing assistant that helps users generate content, personalize marketing, and optimize marketing strategy from customer data. If you've seen older references to the standalone "Email Content Generator," that was Mailchimp's first generative AI email tool, and it has effectively been folded into and rebranded as Intuit Assist functionality.

Alongside the generative assistant, Mailchimp ships a set of predictive and optimization features: predictive analytics, Send Time Optimization and Send Day Optimization, and AI-generated e-commerce automations. In 2026 Intuit added Analytics AI, a native conversational analytics agent, with a companion AI Segment Builder in beta. Some of these features remain in beta, and availability is limited to select countries and to specific plan tiers — so confirm what's actually enabled for your account before you rely on it.

Generating and editing content with Intuit Assist

The most visible piece for day-to-day marketers is generative content. The inline writing tool marketed as Write with AI, powered by Intuit Assist, lets you generate, rewrite, or polish email copy directly within the email editor. Inside the builder you can apply prompts such as "lengthen," "shorten," "change tone," or "correct spelling/grammar," and request rewrites like "rewrite this as a product announcement and make it short."

Intuit Assist can also kick off content on its own. When a new product is added through a connected commerce integration, it can automatically generate a new product announcement email — including subject line, calls-to-action, and product imagery in the brand's identity. Separately, Mailchimp's AI-generated marketing automations build multi-step campaigns with personalized email content for use cases such as welcoming new contacts, recovering abandoned carts, and re-engaging lost customers.

Predictive and analytics features

Beyond writing, Mailchimp leans on data science for timing and targeting. Send Time Optimization uses engagement-pattern data to predict when a contact is most likely to open an email within 24 hours of the selected date, and is available on the Standard plan or higher. Mailchimp's predictive analytics identifies which customers are most likely to purchase and recommends actions to convert those opportunities into revenue.

The newer additions are analytical and conversational. Analytics AI, announced May 28, 2026, is a native conversational analytics agent that lets you ask plain-language questions and receive next-step recommendations connecting marketing activity to revenue, with a companion AI Segment Builder in beta. Earlier, in June 2024, Intuit Mailchimp previewed a Revenue Intelligence system that uses predictive and generative AI to recommend customer segments to target by predicting conversion rates and revenue opportunity.

Reading these features through a bounded-autonomy lens

Most of what's above is genuinely useful, but notice the spectrum. Drafting an email or surfacing a likely-to-purchase segment is one thing — you review the output before anything ships. Automatically generating and queueing a product announcement, or building a multi-step automation, is a step further: the system is taking actions, not just making suggestions.

That's where we'd apply a guardrail-driven evaluation. For each AI feature, trace its Trigger, Action, and Impact: what event sets it off, what it actually does, and what changes for your contacts as a result. The goal isn't to avoid automation — it's to make sure every action is visible and reversible, and that the AI reasons inside limits you set rather than behaving like a black box you can't inspect or undo.

  • Trigger: Is it you clicking "generate," or an upstream event like a new product or an abandoned cart?
  • Action: Does the AI draft for your review, or send and schedule on its own?
  • Impact: Who receives the result, and can you roll it back if it's wrong?

Questions to ask before you commit

Because several of these capabilities are in beta and gated by country and plan tier, your first job is confirming what you can actually use. Check whether Intuit Assist, Analytics AI, the AI Segment Builder, and AI-generated automations are available on your plan and in your region — the marketing page and the feature you log into may not line up.

Then decide where you want a human in the loop. Generative drafting and predictive scoring are low-risk to adopt because you stay in control of what goes out. Auto-generated emails and automations deserve a closer look at approval steps before they're allowed to act on live audiences. Map each feature to how much autonomy you're comfortable granting, and treat that as a deliberate choice rather than a default.

Last reviewed June 2026. Mailchimp updates its AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on the vendor's own site.

Start here

Before you turn on Intuit Assist's automations, know where you stand

Evaluating Mailchimp's AI is really a question about how much autonomy your team is ready to grant. The free Readiness Score tells you in 4 minutes, no login — so you can adopt the generative features with confidence and scope the automated ones carefully.

Get your free Readiness Score →

articles.title: Braze AI Features: How BrazeAI Powers Lifecycle Messaging
articles.h1: Braze AI Features: How BrazeAI Powers Lifecycle Messaging
articles.metaDescription: A buyer's guide to Braze AI features: how BrazeAI agents, Decisioning Studio, Operator, and the predictive suite power cross-channel lifecycle messaging.
articles.ogTitle: Braze AI Features: How BrazeAI Powers Lifecycle Messaging
articles.ogDescription: What BrazeAI actually does — agents, Decisioning Studio, Operator, predictive and intelligence suites — and how to evaluate it through a bounded-autonomy lens.
articles.heroLede: Braze now markets its AI under a single umbrella, BrazeAI. Here's what each piece does, what's still in beta, and how to evaluate it before you buy.
articles.bodyHtml:
What BrazeAI is today

Braze is a cross-channel lifecycle messaging platform, and its AI and machine-learning capabilities are now marketed under a single umbrella brand: BrazeAI™. This is a rename. As of a June 2023 announcement, Braze branded these capabilities "Sage AI by Braze," but the "Sage" name no longer appears on Braze's product pages, documentation, or its 2025-2026 announcements.

The current lineup was largely unveiled at Braze's Forge 2025 event in September 2025 and expanded into 2026. It spans three layers: agentic AI that can take action inside the platform, a reinforcement-learning decisioning engine, and the underlying predictive and intelligence machine-learning features that have long powered Braze campaigns.

Agents, Operator, and the Agent Console

BrazeAI™ Agents are AI-powered helpers built inside Braze. Per Braze's documentation, they can generate content such as subject lines and in-product copy, make decisions that route users through Canvas paths, and enrich data by calculating or generating catalog and profile field values. Agents can run on a Braze-provided model (Google Gemini, shown as "Auto") or on a customer's own connected providers, including OpenAI, Anthropic, Google Gemini, and Databricks Mosaic.

BrazeAI Operator™ is an in-dashboard AI assistant that can take direct action in Braze — building campaigns, writing copy, and answering data questions. Braze announced it at Forge 2025, with a Limited Feature Beta in October 2025 and a Full Feature Beta in January 2026. BrazeAI Agent Console™ lets brands create custom agents for content generation, data enrichment, and intelligent orchestration within Braze Canvas and Catalogs; it entered private beta in September 2025.

Decisioning Studio and the underlying ML

BrazeAI Decisioning Studio™ is an AI decisioning engine built on reinforcement learning. It makes 1:1 personalization decisions — channel, message, creative, offer, and frequency — to optimize a business goal. It represents the integration of OfferFit's AI Decisioning Engine into Braze, based on technology Braze agreed to acquire in March 2025 for $325 million and completed acquiring on June 2, 2025.

Beneath the agentic layer sit Braze's documented machine-learning suites. The Predictive Suite includes Predictive Churn (identifying users at risk of churning) and Predictive Events (forecasting the likelihood a user takes a future action). The Intelligence Suite includes Intelligent Timing (an optimal send time per user), Intelligent Channel (the best channel per user), and Intelligent Selection (automatically shifting traffic toward better-performing message variants). Braze also offers a BrazeAI Model Context Protocol (MCP) Server, announced in beta around Forge 2025, that connects Braze data to external AI clients such as Claude Desktop and Cursor for conversational campaign insights.

Evaluating BrazeAI through a bounded-autonomy lens

BrazeAI mixes two distinct kinds of capability, and the distinction matters when you evaluate it. The predictive and intelligence features score and rank — they tell you who's likely to churn or when to send. Operator, Agents, and the Agent Console act: they build campaigns, write copy, route users, and enrich profiles. The more a feature acts on your behalf, the more you need to see exactly what it did and be able to undo it.

We call that guardrail-driven automation: AI should reason and act only inside limits you set, with every action visible and reversible. As you trial these features, trace each one through Trigger, Action, Impact — what fires the agent, what it's allowed to do, and how you measure and reverse the result. Ask where the human-in-the-loop checkpoints sit, whether agent actions are logged and auditable, and what happens when an agent decides wrong.

One concrete buyer caution: many of the flagship agentic features above — Operator and Agent Console in particular — were in beta as of late 2025 and early 2026. Beta features can change scope, behavior, or availability. Confirm the general-availability status of any specific capability at the time you buy, rather than assuming a demo reflects what ships.

Last reviewed June 2026. Braze updates its AI capabilities frequently — confirm current specifics on the vendor's own site.

Start here

Before you turn an agent loose on your lifecycle messaging

BrazeAI can act inside your campaigns — but only you can decide which actions belong inside your guardrails. The free Readiness Score shows where your team is ready to delegate to AI and where you still need a human in the loop. 4 minutes, no login.

Get your free Readiness Score →

  • Predictive vs. agentic AI in marketing — the exact split between BrazeAI's scoring features and its acting agents
  • AI agents in your CRM — what to demand before agents act on your customer data
  • How to evaluate AI marketing automation platforms — a framework for cutting through beta-stage feature demos

_removedOrFixed: Fixed: changed 'Variant Selection' to 'Intelligent Selection' in the Intelligence Suite — 'Intelligent Selection' is Braze's actual documented feature name; 'Variant Selection' is not a Braze product name.

Fixed: 'Predictive Events/Purchases' narrowed to 'Predictive Events' to match the Predictive Suite documentation page, which lists Predictive Churn and Predictive Events; avoided implying a single combined feature name.

Fixed: reworded Decisioning Studio origin from 'formerly the standalone product OfferFit by Braze' to 'represents the integration of OfferFit's AI Decisioning Engine into Braze,' matching the Forge 2025 press release language and avoiding an unverified product name.
Fixed: 'optimize a business KPI' changed to 'optimize a business goal' to avoid metric-flavored phrasing while preserving meaning.

Fixed: MCP Server timing softened from 'announced in beta at Forge 2025' to 'announced in beta around Forge 2025' — the press release states it was announced 'last month' relative to Forge, so it predates the event slightly.

Retained $325 million acquisition price: this is a confirmed, widely reported transaction price (not a performance metric or percentage) and is verifiable on Braze's investor site and the CDP Institute.
